text: The patient is positioned in the lateral decubitus position with the left side down. The surgical exploration reveals a hernia defect in the posterior aspect of the right diaphragm. A large amount of omentum and colon is visible in the right chest cavity, which is reduced, exposing a seven by five centimeter hernia defect. The right chest cavity is fully visualized, with no peritoneal hernia sac present.

The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver and exposes the left lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect through retroperitoneal dissection, reflecting the right kidney medially. The diaphragmatic defect is closed primarily using figure-of-eight permanent sutures, starting medially and progressing laterally towards the chest wall with the endostitch device.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to close the diaphragmatic defect laterally due to increased tension on the primary repair. A coated propylene mesh is placed over the entire defect and tacked into place. The right kidney and liver are repositioned to their normal anatomical locations, covering most of the mesh.
